speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Ben Gerstein. So before we talk about your liberation, I'd just like to ask you a few more details about-- about your pre-war-- your war experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had to give out handouts to the farmers, did you ever run out of-- of things to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pretty well. And that's it. That's all we had left is two or three gold coins, and maybe a ring, a wedding ring, stuff like that. Four days before the liquidation, my father got-- put us on a-- put us-- he went with us. He got a horse and buggy. And he-- we went to that little miniature little working camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the fourth day, he realized that without money, without gold-- money is gold up there. It was only money. There was no money really. He had to go back to the house-- to our house. And we had a hiding place where we always hid a few pieces of gold. Because in Europe in those days, gold was the-- the only thing that you could barter with, unless you had a gold ring, or a watch, or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he decided he had to go back to find that gold. Otherwise he wouldn't have a chance to hide, or payoff a farmer-- or anybody. He decided-- he left us there in that camp. And he decided to go back into the city. That It was on the fourth day after we got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, he was digging a whole night trying to find the gold. Couldn't. At 6 o'clock in the morning, he started to hear some rumble, some noise. He didn't know what the heck it was. He went up quietly into the top of the house, into the attic. And as I mentioned before, it looks over the whole city. He started to see lights all around-- some trucks, just faintly seeing there, in the silhouette, the trucks. And then it hit him. This is the liquidation. They're ringing-- putting a ring around the city, around our area, the ghetto. And that's the liquidation coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: He ran down quickly. He forgot about the gold. And he start running, because the ghetto was still basically asleep at night, right? He knocked on-- on a few doors on his-- on-- on my uncle's, on my, well, in-laws, my grandfather, grandfather [? door, ?] a couple of his friends, whoever he could.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one particular friend, he told us-- that always sticks out in my mind-- he knocked on the door. he yelled to him. He was his best buddy. He says, this is it. This is the night. They're liquidating. Run for your life. And-- and he woke up the family, that this friends of his. And he screamed at them, run. Follow me. And my father was ahead about two or three blocks of ahead of him. He had turned around. He's seen-- he sees him running behind him. And then he hears voices in Ukrainian-- stop or I'll shoot. And my father sort of didn't take any chance. He started zigzagging in behind a gate, and behind a tree, and he kept running. And he looked back, he realized that the voices is behind him. So they were yelling at his friend. And his friend was doing the same thing. And they start shooting. And he saw him being-- being killed right there. So he didn't make it. And-- and that's how he ran away, basically through the ring of the ghetto. That morning they started liquidation. It lasted for three days. And then crawling at night, he didn't move. And one morning he was about, oh, two miles up-- well, pardon me. That was the next morning. Sorry, I'm jumping all over the place. He saw Ukrainian police just outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he quickly ran into a haystack, a little haystack, you know. That's how they used to build like, like this type of thing. So he opened one up and covered himself. And he went into the little haystack. It's just enough to cover maybe a-- maybe a person.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainian police, they saw the track, because you're breaking where it's cut off. I don't know if you're familiar how [INAUDIBLE]. He broke it. They could see right away tracks. So they pushed the haystacks over. And there's my father hiding-- two Ukrainian guys, policemen. And naturally his instinct was that he's not-- they're not going to take him back. And he's-- if they-- he's going to talk to them. If they feel that he's-- they're going to shoot him, he's going to jump one or two of them. He was very strong, my father. And he's going to take one, or he's not going to give up just-- just being shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he talk to them. And he talked to them in Ukrainian. You know who my name is. I'm sure you do, he says. [INAUDIBLE] father and all that. And he talked to them. And what does it do, you know? One said, look, let's turn our backs, forget about it. And the other one was more agitated about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Make a story short, he somehow got him. They turned their backs, and they walked away. And he didn't walk away with his back to them, either. He kept walking backwards, backwards, backwards. And he was far-- until he was far enough. And then he found us on the second night. So there was a close call.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had gone with a horse and buggy back-- back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: On foot. He waked.

speaker: interviewer
text: On foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked for three days. It was I don't know how many kilometers. He walked for three-- walking at [? that ?] night, and-- and just lying low and hiding in the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any of his--

speaker: interviewee
text: To us, to where we were in that little working camp. And then we went into hiding from there to the-- to the Polish guy, and from the Polish guy to the Slovakian guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me ask you a few questions about the-- the work camp, the halfway station.

speaker: interviewee
text: The halfway camp. That wasn't bad. That wasn't bad. They let us work. There was one, two basic, two-- two Germans that ran the camp. There's a few German soldiers. One wasn't bad. And one was, again, if he get drunk stay away from him. They just can't take it [? from ?] the Indians, you know. We found that out for some reason they couldn't get-- He was not a bad guy. All he wanted is work done, and that's it. The other guy was a major. And he was actually born in Russia. And all through his life he was a Russian spy-- a German spy in Russia. When Germany made a pact with Hitler, and they let all of the Germans come out of Russia-- they wanted to come out of Russia and go to Germany-- he was one of them. But he was, before that, he was a spy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he got to like my dad. And he was saying to him, don't stay here. How long are you going [? to live? ?] You think you're going to keep your life here? You're going to be shot for sure. Get out of here. Get going.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says I spent all of my life to be a spy for Germany, but not for that Germany, he says. He almost cried, almost cried with tears. He couldn't believe that this is the Germany that he wasted his lifetime on. And he offered me even ammunition-- a gun and ammunition. But mainly he told him get out of here. Go into hiding. And he convinced my father to do that. Went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your job in the-- in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doing odds and ends. Being, I don't know, 13, 12, 13 years old, they didn't force us heavy work. Just whatever kids could do. Carry wood, carry bread, you know, stuff like that. Nothing horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the living arrangements like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived at that farmer, at one of the farmers that originally took us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the farmhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the farmhouse. He was actually a teacher in a public school there was somewhere around there. Yeah, he lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And food was not scarce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was not bad. Yeah. It was rich, rich farm country. Very rich farm country. All kinds of bread and vegetables. They had all [? of his ?] own vegetable garden. There was no problem with food, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so being a boy of 12 or 13, did you have your bar mitzvah there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We missed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of recognition?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Recognition that you were-- you were coming of age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. My father didn't want to remind me too much of it, because he was sad that we had to-- we couldn't have it, you know? But when you're fighting for your life, that's the least thing-- the last thing you're thinking about. You see? So, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and then we sort of backtrack a little bit, and we-- and now we're going into the-- as I said before-- the liberation, that we got liberated. We got into the city of Lutsk, our own city. Our house was demolished, completely destroyed. So we had no place to live. And besides, we just moved into an empty house. There was a lot of empty homes. So you went back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To yeah, for only about month. And then we understood-- we heard from-- from Russians that the Germans are creating-- are starting a counter-offensive. And we didn't want to be-- get caught. So we decided to go further deep into Russia, back into Russia deeper. In case they come, the counter-offensive, we didn't want to get caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I was saying, there was-- we lived under complete silence for [? 15 ?] months. We could only whisper. We got liberated. We couldn't talk. The Russian was trying to talk to me. And we couldn't. None of us could talk. So either you talked in a whisper, all of a sudden the voice broke, and you went into a boom. [CHUCKLES] It was like are these normal people? You know, they didn't realize what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, then we decided we have to get away and go to the next town, which was 70 kilometers towards the Russia, deeper into Russia. We had no transportation, and it was winter. We walked. Trouble is we couldn't walk. Our leg muscles couldn't keep us. We would stand and walk like we had polio or something for maybe an hour, and then that was it. That was at the most. And we tried to walk 70 kilometers. And we hitchhiked. We-- we did everything, on the back of a truck in the winter time-- if we were lucky. And that only went part way. And they put us on the-- on half-starving horses, anything, anything. So we were on horses, on a truck, on a sleigh. I put my foot at the end of the sleigh. And I hung on for a few kilometers. We somehow struggled, and we made it to the next town. And that's where we were for about seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Rovno, R-O-V-N-O. We heard there was a Russian-- a Czechoslovakian battalion. They created battalions, the Russians, Polish, or Slovakian, Romanian battalions. And they were all put together under one huge command, but run by their own generals fighting the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I heard-- we had nothing to eat, you know. There was no organization or anything. So I went to that Czechoslovakian camp, the army, and asked for a job. And they gave me a little job in the kitchen. Then they asked-- started asking me question. I told them I was Jewish. I was a survivor. Who else do you have? A mother, a father, a sister, a cousin. So where do they-- I said, well, we all-- we have no work. We have no food. So every night they used to give me a kettle full of soup, and food, and bread. And that's how we survived for about two months. You know. And then the general talked to us, and gave my father a job. And that lasted for about eight months. So this is still in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was still in 1945. Then the Jewish organizations tried to get organized. And they got in touch with us. And they're going to help us. Because everybody wanted to get out of there, out of the Holocaust area, just out of Europe, out of there. Originally was thought Israel, of course. But then we got in touch with our family. In Germany we got in touch. But in the meantime, going back here, the organization got us papers as Greek immigrants. So you're not-- we didn't understand Polish, Jewish, or whatever. Only Greek. [CHUCKLING] I didn't know a word of Greek. They taught us to say two words in Greek, or three words, in case they tried to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those words?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. And we were all lined up in the-- at the Polish border. And-- and one supposedly knew a little bit of Greek, and a slight, slight little bit of German. Not Polish. He was Polish. But they didn't understand German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they frisked us, make sure that we don't carry any money with us or anything. And we showed them Greek passports. And they talked-- they tried to catch us. They tried to talk to us in Polish a little bit. And-- and they didn't. Anyway, they let us go through that border, through the Polish border into Czechoslovakia. It was a little easier to get into-- we told them we had just going by Czechoslovakia to go back to Greece.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed in Prague one night. Another organization took us over, also Jews, and they now smuggled us on our stomachs and our bellies at night through the Czechoslovakian border, out of Czechoslovakia now and into West Germany. That's where we're heading. That's how we got to West Germany. There they put us into camps, every survivor there. They-- they spread us over campus. That's how we wound up in Fohrenwald. And we stayed there for close to three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Fohrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fohrenwald, it's near-- have you heard of Garmisch-Partenkirchen? It's between Garmisch and Munich, right in between those two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it in Bavaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Bavaria, yeah. Wolfratshausen is about two kilometers from-- from that little camp. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say about 1,500, roughly. It used to be a-- a German-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was built for Germany, for German soldiers. It was a-- I don't know what the heck they did. But it was like a temporary little village-- little town that they built. They made a little town for-- for the war. And the Germans were working, I don't know, maybe on the rocket. I don't know. There was that type of thing. But there were German people that lived there before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there in-- in fall of '55.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. Fall of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my cousin-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with my cousin and my-- yeah. Just with my cousin. My aunt, as we were running back, she got a lift on a truck that forgot to let-- let her off. As we were going to [? Rowne, ?] they forgot to let her off, that she was in the back. She wound up in Kiev, 150 kilometers further. That's where we-- that's how we got separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what does she do from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she met a Russian soldier just out of the army there. And they married. And they came to Canada also. And she's now in Canada. Yeah, so it's-- it's funny how things work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we stayed in Fohrenwald until we were just about ready to leave for New York. We got papers to New York. We applied for both Canada and New York. My father's relatives were in New York, and we were in this Bremenhaven. That's a port city. You've heard of Bremen? This is Bremenhaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had to make a lottery, because of only a certain-- two boats were supposed to show off to take us, but only one made it for some reason. And they chose lotteries-- from each family one person or two people. We wound up with one ticket only from our family to go on that first boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father put me on the boat. I was the youngest. He wanted out, out of there. And I was in New York for about six months-- OK I'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to backtrack a tiny bit and ask you about-- about-- what is it called? Fohrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fohrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you about life there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Fohrenwald was, as I say, like a little subdivision, of a one story or two story little-- little houses, little semi-detached. And the UNRRA supported us, United Nations Relief or-- Association.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and a little bit, we got in touch by that time with our family from-- from Canada. And they sent us a couple of dollars. But a couple of dollars went a long way then, you know? So we were getting a little bit from that, plus food, and lodging, you know, immediate needs supported the UNRRA-- by the UNRRA. And did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I went to school. I was going, in Munich. Actually, my father made me-- made me-- he-- he made me go to school to learn a trade-- dentistry. Dentistry, you had to go through college, you know, [INAUDIBLE] and all of that. He decided to have me go through first as a dental technician. Then when we get to Canada, then if I wanted to I could become a dentist or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were commuting to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Munich. And I-- no and I lived with [? her. I had-- ?] later on I started to-- it was too hard to commute every day. So we had a room, a friend of ours, my father's friend's son. And my father's friend was a dentist. I guess that's how that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his son and I were-- we had a little room. And we were going to school. And on the weekend we used to come back and into Fohrenwald. And what were your parents doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing really. Nothing, there was no job to-- to have or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waited for the papers to come through so we could immigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was not going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of us did anything. No, No, she-- school was in Munich. And she was already three years older somehow. She-- she didn't want to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'd like to ask you some more questions about when you were in hiding. What were you talking about every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a problem. How do you keep yourself in hiding night and day in one spot with your knees bent, crunched? We couldn't straighten out. Everybody had to walk away on one side of the room for a person to stretch. As a matter of fact, I remember we were nine at that particular-- the haystack. Eight of us got typhus. Eight of nine got typhus. Just my mother did not get typhus. And I don't know how that works. And she looked after eight people. Basically making right under us. We didn't know what we were doing. We were all in high fever. She had to clean us, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have the tools to clean you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Whatever ?] was [? known. ?] She didn't have the tools. We used straw. We used anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you delirious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were delirious. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you sick for?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the typhus, I'd say eight days, something like that. Seven, eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for the rest of the time, do you remember how you kept yourself occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Basically you slept. You were always thinking of food. You were hungry. And talking, you couldn't. It's hard to have a conversation when you have to whisper. So it was-- it was a long six months. Very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special games that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dark. You were in darkness. Couldn't have a light or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: And besides, how you were scared like anything to light a match when you're in a haystack, or in a straw stack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It didn't help, because if they heard voices, whether you speak Polish or Jewish it doesn't mean anything. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were never discovered when you were in the straw stack?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were never discovered. Oh no, I wouldn't be telling you that story if we were discovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any news from the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because even the farmers, they had a radio that half the time it didn't work, and how far did it carry, you know? And that was the problem, rumor of rumor. Well, I heard-- I heard that the Russians starting to beat back the Germans. We heard. We don't know. Somebody told them that somebody said that somebody mentioned, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So never any concrete news from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And that was the scary part. We didn't know how long. And that's why the farmer, the Czech person, he-- he didn't know how long. How long. And that was it was the problem. Two months, OK. Three months, OK. But how long? And every time they couldn't sleep at night [INAUDIBLE]. The little guy, they had a little guy there of six years old. He didn't know we were there. Every time they send us food, they made sure that they kept him busy, kept him in the house. And that went on for nine months, never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I also wanted to ask you about yellow stars, before you were-- before you went into hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We wore, yeah. Oh yeah. We wore the yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to conceal your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No point in it. In our situation no point.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's nobody that-- that tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the ghetto-- oh yeah, there were some people that tried. They tried. they live with a Polish family, and they said that this is my cousin, come-- came from [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what I mean. But that was very unique, very little. My uncle did, as a matter of fact, my-- my mother's brother. Most of the time he hid out with some Polish friend that he went to school when he was a little boy-- one of them especially to cut papers for him. And he kept him. He was on the-- on the Polish papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was never found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So skipping back ahead then, you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back ahead where I'm still--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a teenager.

speaker: interviewee
text: A manger?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're about-- you're about to be put on the boat to New York. What are your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're about to be put on the boat to New York. What are your thoughts then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Before-- see we got a little bit jumped forth and back. It happens to be on my mind now. With the manger, we didn't finish that story. Because this is interesting. This is as close as we came to-- to being done with. As we lay in that little place, in that little four by four, we started to get short of air, because there was no ventilation. It was just running down, they came into the barn, and they go. Unfortunately they set-- they set up a little mini camp in the barn. It was a huge barn. OK. And we could hear the voices. We could hear the Germans sitting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's getting less oxygen, less oxygen. We are starting to choke for air. Now my father can't speak anymore. He's starting to choke. He would send us-- every time somebody [? yells ?] there was a little crack, you know? A little crack. And we would [INHALING] breathe in the air. And then he would send up my sister there. And then he would hardly be able to make it up. Then there was decision-making. Do we open it up, and-- and whatever they do with us? Or do we die there? OK. So we decided we were going to die there. We weren't going to open it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were already starting to get unconscious. We didn't realize they left the-- the barn. They finally they packed up. They had a lunch or whatever, and then they left. And that's when the farmer came running in. He knew there was no ventilation. And he opened up that door and he found us all unconscious. But when that air hit us, somehow slowly he reached down, pulled us out-- the kids first and then-- and we started to come to. Another-- another three minutes and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had passed out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We passed out, yeah. Yeah. So that-- and that-- that was the closest thing we came. And what? I think another two months, a month and a half to liberation after that incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have more room in this second barn than in the haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We were up on top. Most the time we were up on top. And there's all kinds of room. We could go dancing there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK we'll finish this, the third tape here.
